channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Data centers face climate risks; Job postings soar; Alaska may get more developments
item.description: Floods and other events tied to climate change may hinder financing and insurance of data centers, according to First Street. (Getty Images).
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Warm, dry and breezy conditions raise fire risk statewide today | Montana Weather | kulr8.com
item.description:
Our team is tracking a warm summer day today with light and dry weather impacts. Minor smoke impacts could be expected in areas across Missoula, Great Falls and Lewistown throughout today.

With these conditions in mind, there could be an elevated fire risk for a majority of the state today due to how hot, dry and breezy it is.

Forecasted lows for tonight are in the 50s and 60s with western Montana expecting cooling temps. Forecasted highs for tomorrow are in the 80s and 90s.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Europe's heatwave disrupts energy as UK links 42% of deaths to climate change - MSN
item.description: This aligns with broader climate risk assessments urging policymakers to prepare for concurrent disasters that can overwhelm existing coping capacity.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Labor's dud energy projects risk green goals - AFR
item.description:
Clean energy investors and industry experts are pushing for changes to the government’s flagship renewables subsidy program, as deteriorating market conditions raise the prospect that over half of the projects in the scheme’s early rounds will never be built.

The multibillion-dollar Capacity Investment Scheme is a key policy to help achieve Labor’s 82 per cent 2030 renewables target by allowing clean energy companies to bid for government support to build new projects, but has so far struggled to get shovels in the ground.

Loading...
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.description: Cotality New Zealand chief data officer Craig Dargusch said while buyers faced undeniable climate risk, immediate financial pressures were dictating ...

item.title: As climate risks grow, China centres disaster preparedness on saving lives | Borneo Post Online
item.description: BEIJING (July 20): This year's flood season has brought unusually severe weather across China, with torrential rains, floods, typhoons and other ...

item.title: Insurers' climate carve-out may erode public confidence, officials warn - Newsroom
item.description:
Exempting health and life insurers from a reporting regime for climate-related risks could erode public confidence in the system and weaken its integrity and effectiveness, officials have warned.

The minister overseeing the changes has argued they are a “common-sense” move to focus on the sectors with the highest levels of climate risk – but one high-profile group of lawyers says it is another example of the Government ignoring evidence-based policy.

Subscribe to continue reading

  • Unlimited access Newsroom Pro articles
  • Bonus subscription for a friend or family member
  • Exclusive subscriber-only emails
  • Access to post and read comments on articles
  • A daily morning email of news and insights curated by Newsroom Pro editorial team

item.title: Africa climate disaster: Warnings have improved but preparedness still lags
item.description: Weather, climate and water-related hazards have accounted for more than 90 per cent of recorded disasters globally over the past five decades,
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: LETTER: Municipal election candidates should include climate change - Guelph Today
item.description:
GuelphToday received the following letter in regards to wildfire smoke:

As the air is filled with wildfire smoke, I hear lots of conversations about not exercising outdoors, managing the air quality in our homes, and evacuation plans for communities in Northern Ontario.

But there is a conversation I'm not hearing. What about the root causes of the increased numbers of wildfires? There has been little mention of global warming resulting from accumulated greenhouse gases, and how this reality unpins the fires, floods, heat domes, atmospheric rivers and other weather-related risks we are experiencing. There has been even less said about how we collectively and individually contribute to this problem. Or what we can do to solve it.

Instead there is a hand-wringing conversation as if wildfires and floods are an inevitability that we just need to adjust to. Stop right there. We need to prevent and mitigate the cause of these problems. We (collectively) solved acid rain. We (collectively) solved the ozone hole issue. And if we put our attention on it, society can at least hold global warming at its current state, and not just allow this problem to run rampant.

What can we each do? Here are some ideas to start, in no particular order. Stop using a gas powered lawn mower and find an electric one. Plant lots of native species and lots of trees - they are carbon sinks. If you travel, reduce the number of flights you take. If you can afford it, buy an EV. If you can't, can you reduce the number of trips you take? Or use a bike or walk? Or take the bus? Or join a car pool?

Vote for politicians who factor climate realities into their policies and legislation. There is a municipal election on Oct. 26 - which of Guelph's mayoral candidates will stand up to address local climate issues?

Eat less red meat. Join community groups that are working to build a clean and sustainable energy system. Email, call or visit your elected officials and let them know you want local, provincial and federal policy to address climate risks, and to build clean energy networks. Invest your money in industries, business and organizations that are actually working to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, not those that are creating the problem. Purchase items mindfully, and repair your stuff rather than replace it whenever possible. This list goes on.

Don't waste time wringing your hands, take action. Roll up your sleeves and line up your behaviours to not just manage living with smoke in the air today, but to actually reduce the overall risk in the long term.

Adrienne Crowder
Guelph
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
